note_data.title: Group 2
keyPoints: Group 2 elements are **alkaline earth metals** with electronic configuration ns² where n is the outer shell
Group 2 elements show **metallic bonding** and form **ionic compounds** with oxidation state +2
**Atomic radius increases** down Group 2 due to additional electron shells
**First ionisation energy decreases** down Group 2 as outer electrons are further from nucleus and more shielded
**Reactivity increases** down Group 2 because ionisation energies decrease making electron loss easier
**Thermal stability of carbonates increases** down Group 2: MgCO₃ < CaCO₃ < SrCO₃ < BaCO₃
**Thermal stability of nitrates increases** down Group 2 following the same pattern as carbonates
Group 2 hydroxides become **more soluble** down the group: Mg(OH)₂ < Ca(OH)₂ < Sr(OH)₂ < Ba(OH)₂
Group 2 sulfates become **less soluble** down the group: MgSO₄ > CaSO₄ > SrSO₄ > BaSO₄
**Flame test colours**: Mg - no colour, Ca - brick red, Sr - crimson red, Ba - green

definitions.definition: Group 2 elements that form alkaline solutions when their oxides react with water
Breaking down of a compound by heat into two or more simpler substances
The ability of a cation to distort the electron cloud of an anion
The enthalpy change when one mole of an ionic solid is formed from gaseous ions under standard conditions

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Use trends to suggest properties of unknown Group 2 compounds
State similarities and differences with specific examples

modelAnswer.question: Explain why the thermal stability of Group 2 carbonates increases down the group. [3]
modelAnswer.answer: Cation charge density decreases down Group 2. This is because ionic radius increases while charge remains +2. Smaller, more highly charged cations have greater polarising power and distort the carbonate ion more, making decomposition easier.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Cation charge density decreases down the group
Mark 2: Ionic radius increases while charge stays the same
Mark 3: Higher charge density causes greater polarisation of carbonate ion

commonMistakes: Never say 'atomic size' — always say 'atomic radius' or 'ionic radius'
Students often confuse solubility trends: hydroxides increase, sulfates decrease down Group 2
Don't say 'stability increases because atoms get bigger' — explain using charge density and polarising power
Never write M²⁺ in equations — always specify the actual metal (Mg²⁺, Ca²⁺, etc.)
Students forget that MgCO₃ and Mg(NO₃)₂ decompose at relatively low temperatures compared to other Group 2 compounds
examinerTips: Thermal stability questions appear frequently - learn the charge density explanation
Always give specific examples when discussing trends (e.g. Mg vs Ba)
Solubility questions often test both hydroxides and sulfates - learn both trends
Flame tests are commonly tested - memorise colours exactly as given in syllabus
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Born-Haber cycle showing lattice enthalpy trends, or apparatus for thermal decomposition of carbonates with gas syringe to collect CO₂
